data.title: Brainstorm feasibility reviewer skipped via false rationalization of Jira API dependency
data.description:
## Summary

During brainstorm for the Ticket System Boundary Consolidation epic, the feasibility reviewer dispatch was skipped with the rationalization: 'no external integration signal (Jira REST API is already used by the bridge; issue properties is a new endpoint of the same API).'

This rationalization is incorrect. The epic explicitly depends on the Jira REST API issue properties endpoint (PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/properties/{propertyKey}), which:
- Is a distinct API surface from the existing bridge endpoints (issue CRUD, comments, links)
- May require different OAuth scopes or permissions than what the bridge currently uses
- Has different rate limiting characteristics under Atlassian's new points-based system (effective March 2026)
- Has never been used in this codebase before

The brainstorm skill's feasibility reviewer trigger condition states: 'epic references external integrations (third-party APIs, CI/CD, infrastructure) OR first-time usage of internal platform features.' The issue properties endpoint is a first-time usage of a third-party API endpoint — the fact that the same vendor (Atlassian) provides it does not make it 'already used.'

## Rationalization Produced

'Feasibility reviewer check: no external integration signal (Jira REST API is already used by the bridge. Issue properties is a new endpoint of the same API) and no first-time internal platform features (pre-commit hooks, event types, and CLI subcommands are all established patterns). Skipping feasibility review.'

## Impact

The feasibility reviewer would have caught permission/scope risks, rate limiting concerns, and API availability questions that the red team scenario analysis later surfaced independently. Skipping it created a gap in the scrutiny pipeline.

## Suggested Fix

Tighten the brainstorm skill's feasibility reviewer trigger logic to clarify that 'already used' means the specific endpoint or API surface has been exercised in the codebase, not merely that the same vendor's API is used elsewhere. A new endpoint of an existing vendor API is still a first-time external integration.
